name: On the Matter of Magecraft and the City: A Summary for the Lords of Waterdeep
author: Khelben Arunsun ("the Elder")
description:
Khelben Arunsun the Elder briefed Waterdeep's Lords on arcane regulation — which magics threaten civic order, which guilds must be watched, and why some spells are best left unnamed in open council.

The summary remains classified in many editions; leaked copies circulate with whole pages replaced by single word: "No."

name: On Goblins: My Life Among the Conquering Host
author: Volothamp Geddarm
description:
Volo recounts weeks embedded with a goblin war band — food, hierarchy, superstition, and the uncomfortable truth that many "conquering hosts" are starving families with stolen swords.

Elminster appended a warning that sympathy must not blur vigilance; goblins still bite.

name: Order of the Gauntlet: On Matters of Law and Justice
author: Order of the Gauntlet scribes
description:
A doctrinal text for Gauntlet knights — when justice requires the sword, when mercy serves the righteous, and how to investigate evil without becoming what you hunt.

Frontier chapters address orc raids, Zhent slavery, and cooperation with Flaming Fist garrisons that do not share the Order's oaths.

name: One Warrior's Life
author: Azlundar, Lion of Neverwinter
description:
One Warrior's Life — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Azlundar, Lion of Neverwinter is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1289 DR.

Later hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.

name: Of the Wonders of Magery
author: Albaerum the Sage of Neverwinter
description:
Of the Wonders of Magery — A treatise on magic as it is actually practiced — wards, reputations, and the price of mispronunciation. Albaerum the Sage of Neverwinter is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1320s DR.

Complete copies are rare; excerpted versions circulate among mages who insist the missing pages do not matter.

name: On the Worship of Umberlee
author: Unknown
description:
On the Worship of Umberlee — Devotional and doctrinal writing circulated among temples and faithful households along the trade routes. Library catalogues list no certain author; the work is dated to before 1372 DR.

Temple libraries hold the cleanest editions; roadside chapbooks may omit verses the clergy prefer not to recite aloud.

name: Observations of the Tuigan Historian
author: Koja of Khazari
description:
Observations of the Tuigan Historian — A chronicle preserved in northern and coastal scriptoria — dates, names, and the sort of detail merchants pretend not to need. Koja of Khazari is credited in library catalogues; the work is dated to 1362 DR.

Later hands added marginal dates and crossed out names; historians still argue which revisions were propaganda.
